note: This is a design-grammar knowledge base for the LLM layout planner, not a set of selectable templates. The LLM references these to understand when each composition idea applies, then generates a new grid-based layout_plan tailored to the actual input.

patterns.when_to_use: One strong image with short or no accompanying text. The image carries the full page (or spread half) with no competing text block, often used as a section/chapter opener.
One image with a moderate amount of explanatory text. The image sits in the upper portion of the page and text follows below it as an independent block, never overlapping.
One image paired with text of roughly equal visual weight, read side by side. Works well when the page needs a stable, symmetric two-column feel.
Same as single_left_image_right_text but with reading priority given to the text column on the left; the image supports/illustrates rather than leads.
Two images of equal importance (e.g. a comparison or a before/after), placed side by side with equal size, with little or no text competing for attention.
Two images shown together at equal size, with an explanatory text block placed below them as a separate, independent zone.
One dominant (hero) image plus one or more smaller supporting images. Used when the images are not all equally important — one clearly leads the story.
Four images of similar visual weight and similar aspect ratios, arranged in a stable 2x2 grid, with a text block occupying the remaining space.
One large hero image combined with a small grid of secondary images, for 3-6 images where one clearly stands out and the rest form a supporting set.
Several images of similar importance and similar ratios arranged as a gallery/archive grid on one page, with text as a separate independent block (same page or facing page).
Many images (typically 4-6) combined with long body text: dedicate one full page/spread-half to an image gallery, and a separate page/spread-half purely to reading text, so neither is compressed.
